verbatim_text: So what this means is that, okay, so we have sort of compared roughly at least what learning the algorithm with the right surrogate reward and sampler compares how that compares with trying to use curated data. And that sort of ends the second part of the talk where we're looking at some practical design choices for a sampler and reward that define the training objective, you maximize that objective. And now we'll try and apply that in a real world application in the third part of the talk, which is an application on theorem proving. And here we'll go into the details of the QDnano result that we saw earlier on. In general, my work on training models to adapt at test time, I've deployed it in a few applications, including theorem proving, online web agents, which involve interactions with the web now is a little bit different than theorem proving. And also generally in training small models to reason for longer and improved performance in math. But I'll focus here on the theorem proving result. So the setting here is that you want to train a four billion parameter LLM. It's a small LLM to prove theorems. What do we have for this? We have a data set of just 5000 training problems. What is the problem? A problem is a tuple of this theorem and a grading rubric, which is used to grade proofs for that particular theorem. Now, these problems are sourced from this art of problem solving data set roughly.
